---
title: "Table Interchange"
subtitle: "Convert typed temporal objects to pandas tables without inventing missing timing decisions."
sidebar: concepts
---

## Why tables are an export format, not the primary object

A pandas table is convenient for inspection, plotting, statistics, and file
export. It is less good at preserving the natural structure of every feature.
A dense matrix, a list of bounded events, and a set of tracked objects do not
become the same kind of data merely because all three can be written as rows.

`natural_features` therefore keeps typed feature objects in memory and creates
tables when you ask for them. The table is a rectangular exchange format; the
typed object remains the source of truth.

## How each object becomes rows

| Input object | Table function | Meaning of one output row |
|---|---|---|
| `FeatureSeries` | `as_feature_table()` | One feature value at one time |
| `EventSeries` | `as_event_table()` | One event with its onset, offset, label, and optional fields |
| `TrackSeries` | `as_track_table()` | One tracked feature value for one track at one time |
| Any of the three | `as_temporal_table()` | Dispatch to the matching conversion above |

Suppose a `FeatureSeries` has 100 times and 13 feature columns. Its long table
has 1,300 rows: one row for each time-and-feature pair. This shape avoids
pretending that feature names are part of the time axis.

Every temporal table includes `time_reference`, the name of the clock used by
its time columns. Rows with temporal extent also include onset and offset
columns. Origin-information columns can be included so the name of the
feature-computing method and the fingerprint of its settings survive export.

## Object tables use a common vocabulary

`as_object_table()` is for event detections and tracks that should share
object-oriented columns. It normalizes fields such as `object_id`,
`object_type`, `onset_s`, `offset_s`, confidence, and geometry. This makes it
possible to compare object-producing extractors without erasing their extra
fields.

```{python}
from natural_features.core.interchange import OBJECT_CANONICAL_COLUMNS

OBJECT_CANONICAL_COLUMNS[:10]
```

An extra event column is included only when it has one value per event. A
mismatched length is rejected because the library cannot know which event the
extra values belong to.

## Long and wide merges make different promises

A long merge stacks rows while retaining names that identify their source. It
can represent dense features, events, and tracks without requiring them to
share columns.

A wide merge places several feature columns beside one another on shared rows.
That operation is defined only for `FeatureSeries` outputs because event and
track rows do not have the same rectangular meaning. A wide merge across
different clocks also requires a temporal context, a target clock, and a join
policy. Without those choices, equal-looking timestamps may refer to different
origins.

## Table conversion does not align or resample

The table helpers preserve the rows they receive. They do not interpolate a
feature to a new rate, infer a mapping between clocks, or guess how words map
to frames. Use `ExtractFeaturesResult.align_to()` first when you need a recorded
relationship to a word, event, frame, or custom timeline.

When the next analysis can accept native grids, keep the objects in a
`FeatureBundle` and hand over a temporal payload instead of forcing a wide
table. This preserves timing until the analysis is ready to choose its own
grid.

Requesting table output requires pandas. Missing pandas, unsupported object
types, incompatible extra-column lengths, and under-specified cross-clock wide
merges produce errors rather than partial tables.

## See also

[Export events to a table](../cookbook/event-table-export.qmd) · [Merge feature tables](../cookbook/feature-table-merge.qmd) · [Align features to a timeline](../cookbook/timeline-alignment.qmd)
